summary: DHH highlights a Java developer named Sean who has been working with Ruby on Rails and is frustrated by the dismissive reactions from the Java community toward Trails (an attempt to recreate Rails in Java). Sean pushes back against the 'too simple for enterprise' crowd, arguing his enterprise experience shows Rails is capable. DHH welcomes Java developers crossing over and acknowledges Rails' debt to Java frameworks like Hibernate and Struts. He closes with a pun suggesting the enterprise world should look beyond J2EE.
key_points: The Java community's knee-jerk dismissal of Rails as 'too simple for enterprise' is counterproductive and embarrasses its own members
Rails drew direct inspiration from Java frameworks like Hibernate, Struts, WebWork, and Tapestry
Enterprise experience shouldn't be used as an excuse to reject new languages and approaches
Cross-pollination between Java and Ruby communities benefits Rails development
money_quotes: The most prevalent is the 'cute, but too simple and can't possibly handle my manly enterprise needs' crowd.
I don't use it as an excuse to toss out new languages any more than I would insist that an enterprise system needs to stay on a mainframe.
The Java community certainly has tons to teach Ruby on Rails.
If only more of them could come to terms with the fact that J2EE isn't the last stop on the enterprise ride — the rails go on.
key_insight: Enterprise complexity is not an excuse to dismiss simpler tools — real enterprise experience often reveals that simplicity scales better than the Java community's defensive gatekeeping would suggest.
